title: Topic 37: Cheating and Toxicity: The Correlation Between Bad Behavior Types
content:
_ABSTRACT_

This paper delves into the unholy matrimony of cheating and toxicity in online gaming, a union more cursed than a level 1 player wandering into a max-level zone. We explore the psychological underpinnings of why the player who just wall-hacked their way to victory is also the one composing a symphony of insults in the chat. Through a rigorous, yet surprisingly hilarious, analysis of existing research and real-world examples, we argue that these two forms of bad behavior are not distant cousins but rather twisted siblings, born from the same womb of insecurity, frustration, and a desperate need for validation. The findings suggest that understanding this correlation is not just crucial for game developers seeking to cleanse their virtual realms, but also for anyone who has ever wondered why some people turn into digital monsters the moment they log on. In the end, we conclude that the line between “git gud” and “get a life” is often blurred by a cocktail of anonymity, competition, and the timeless human desire to not suck at video games.

_INTRODUCTION_

It is a truth universally acknowledged that a single player in possession of a good internet connection must be in want of a fair game. Yet, this noble pursuit of digital sportsmanship is often shattered by the unceremonious arrival of two of the online world’s most unwelcome guests: the cheater and the toxic player. The former, a phantom with an aimbot, glides through walls and lands impossible headshots with the grace of a drunken ballerina. The latter, a wordsmith of the profane, unleashes a torrent of insults that would make a sailor blush. But what if these two seemingly distinct archetypes of online villainy are, in fact, two sides of the same tarnished coin?

This paper posits that cheating and toxicity in online gaming are not merely concurrent phenomena but are deeply intertwined, stemming from a shared set of psychological drivers. We will explore the dark corners of the human psyche that lead a player to not only break the rules but also to verbally eviscerate their opponents (and sometimes their own teammates) in the process. From the fragile ego that can only be soothed by a string of ill-gotten victories to the frustration-aggression hypothesis playing out in real-time, we will dissect the motivations behind this dual-pronged assault on the sanctity of online gaming.

Through a comprehensive review of the existing literature, a deep-dive analysis of the psychological mechanisms at play, and a collection of frankly unbelievable real-world examples, this paper will demonstrate that the correlation between cheating and toxicity is no mere coincidence. It is a predictable, and often inseparable, pairing of behaviors that reveals much about the human condition in the digital age. Our journey will take us from the hallowed halls of academic research to the salt-drenched trenches of online multiplayer, all in an effort to understand why some players just can’t play nice. So, grab your popcorn, mute your mic, and prepare to delve into the fascinating, and often hilarious, world of cheating and toxicity in online gaming.

_LITERATURE REVIEW_

The hallowed halls of academia have not been entirely deaf to the cacophony of online gaming. Researchers, bless their hearts, have donned their digital armor and ventured into the wilds of multiplayer lobbies to understand the strange creatures that inhabit them. Their findings, while often couched in the dry language of scholarly journals, paint a fascinating picture of the cheating and toxic player.

Early research tended to treat cheating and toxicity as separate, if equally unpleasant, phenomena. Studies on cheating often focused on the motivations behind this digital malfeasance. Some scholars, like those behind the study on the “Cheating Mood,” have suggested that cheating can be a form of mood regulation, a way for players to repair their fragile egos after a series of crushing defeats [1]. It’s the digital equivalent of eating a pint of ice cream after a bad day, only with more headshots. Other studies have pointed to a more sinister motivation: a deep-seated need for dominance and a win-at-all-costs mentality [2]. These are the players who believe that “it’s not whether you win or lose, it’s whether you make the other person cry.”

Toxicity, on the other hand, has often been examined through the lens of social psychology. The concept of deindividuation, where the anonymity of the online world strips away social inhibitions, is a recurring theme [3]. When you’re just a username and an avatar, the social contract that governs polite society can feel a bit… optional. This, combined with the frustration-aggression hypothesis, which posits that frustration leads to aggression, creates a fertile breeding ground for toxic behavior [4]. A player who is losing badly is more likely to lash out, and the anonymity of the internet provides the perfect shield.

More recently, however, researchers have begun to connect the dots between these two behaviors. A growing body of literature suggests that cheating and toxicity are not just two separate problems but are often two symptoms of the same underlying disease. A 2024 study on the “Dynamics of Harm in Game Cheating” delved into the toxic culture of cheating communities, finding that the two behaviors often go hand-in-hand [5]. Another study on “Toxic Behavior in Multiplayer Online Games” found that players who witnessed verbal aggression were more likely to engage in toxic behavior themselves, creating a vicious cycle of negativity [6]. This suggests that toxicity is not just an individual problem but a social one, a contagion that can spread through a gaming community like a digital plague.

Furthermore, research has begun to explore the psychological profiles of players who engage in both cheating and toxicity. These studies have found that such players often exhibit a “dark triad” of personality traits: narcissism, Machiavellianism, and psychopathy [7]. In other words, they are more likely to be self-obsessed, manipulative, and lacking in empathy. This is the player who will cheat their way to victory, then spend the next ten minutes gloating about it in the chat, completely oblivious to the fact that they have just ruined the game for everyone else.

In conclusion, the literature suggests that while cheating and toxicity can exist independently, they are often found together, like two peas in a very dysfunctional pod. They are both rooted in a complex interplay of psychological and social factors, from the fragile ego to the anonymous online environment. The research is clear: the player who is willing to break the rules is also more likely to be the one who breaks the spirit of the game. And that, as any gamer will tell you, is the most unforgivable sin of all.

_ANALYSIS_

Now that we’ve waded through the scholarly discourse, it’s time to roll up our sleeves and get our hands dirty in the messy, fascinating, and often hilarious psychology of the cheating, toxic gamer. To truly understand this unholy union, we must dissect the motivations, justifications, and cognitive dissonances that allow a player to simultaneously break the rules and the social contract.

At the heart of the matter lies a concept that any armchair psychologist worth their salt will be familiar with: the fragile ego. The online gaming world, with its leaderboards, kill/death ratios, and constant, unforgiving feedback, is a veritable minefield for the insecure. For some, the sting of defeat is not just a momentary setback but a direct assault on their self-worth. Cheating, in this context, becomes a form of self-preservation. It’s a way to guarantee victory, to silence the nagging voice of inadequacy with a chorus of ill-gotten “pwned.” The aimbot is not just a tool for better accuracy; it’s a crutch for a crippled ego.

But where does the toxicity come in? If cheating is the shield, then toxicity is the sword. The cheater, deep down, knows that their victory is hollow. They know that they didn’t earn it, and they are terrified that everyone else knows it too. This insecurity manifests as a preemptive strike. Before anyone can accuse them of cheating, they unleash a torrent of insults, a smokescreen of vitriol designed to distract and disorient. “U mad, bro?” is not just a taunt; it’s a desperate attempt to reframe the narrative, to turn the focus away from their own illegitimate victory and onto the supposed saltiness of their opponents.

This is where the frustration-aggression hypothesis comes into play, but with a twist. It’s not just the frustration of losing that leads to aggression, but the frustration of *almost* getting away with it. The cheater is in a constant state of low-grade paranoia, a perpetual fear of being discovered. Every accusation, every suspicious glance, is a threat to their carefully constructed facade of skill. The resulting aggression is not just a reaction to losing, but a defense mechanism against being exposed as a fraud.

Furthermore, we must consider the role of cognitive dissonance, the mental discomfort experienced when holding two or more contradictory beliefs. The cheating, toxic player believes, on some level, that they are a skilled gamer. Yet, their actions (cheating) directly contradict this belief. To resolve this dissonance, they engage in a series of mental gymnastics that would impress an Olympic gold medalist. They might tell themselves that “everyone else is doing it,” or that the game is “broken” and they are just “leveling the playing field.” They might even convince themselves that their opponents are so bad that they *deserve* to be cheated on and insulted.

This is where the toxicity becomes a tool of justification. By dehumanizing their opponents, by reducing them to a string of insults and slurs, the cheater makes it easier to justify their own unsportsmanlike behavior. It’s the same psychological mechanism that has been used to justify atrocities throughout history, just on a much smaller, and admittedly more ridiculous, scale. It’s hard to feel guilty about cheating against a “noob,” a “scrub,” or a creative combination of expletives that would make a drill sergeant proud.

In essence, the correlation between cheating and toxicity is not just a coincidence; it’s a symbiotic relationship. The cheating is born of insecurity, and the toxicity is the desperate, flailing attempt to protect that insecurity from the harsh light of reality. The cheater needs the toxicity to justify their actions, and the toxic player needs the cheating to feel powerful. They are two sides of the same counterfeit coin, a currency of unearned victories and unprovoked aggression. And as long as there are fragile egos and anonymous online lobbies, this unholy alliance will continue to thrive, much to the chagrin of anyone who just wants to play a fair game.

_REAL-WORLD EXAMPLES_

Lest you think this discussion of cheating and toxicity is purely theoretical, a mere academic exercise in navel-gazing, let us now turn our attention to the wild, untamed frontier of the real world. Here, amidst the flashing lights of esports tournaments and the dimly lit bedrooms of aspiring pro-gamers, we find a veritable treasure trove of examples that are as hilarious as they are horrifying.

Consider the case of Johnathan “Fatal1ty” Wendel, a man so legendary in the world of first-person shooters that he has his own line of branded computer hardware. In the early 2000s, at the height of his fame, a video surfaced that appeared to show him using an aimbot in a public match. The evidence was damning, the community was in an uproar, and for a brief moment, it seemed as if the king of esports was about to be dethroned. Wendel, for his part, denied the allegations with the pained expression of a man who has just been accused of kicking a puppy. He claimed the video was a fabrication, a smear campaign by his jealous rivals. The truth, as is often the case in these situations, remains shrouded in a fog of speculation and low-resolution video evidence. But the incident serves as a perfect example of the paranoia and suspicion that permeates the competitive gaming scene. Was he cheating? Or was he just that good? The world may never know, but one thing is for sure: the accusation alone was enough to tarnish his golden reputation.

Then there is the cautionary tale of the professional “Counter-Strike” player who was caught cheating live on stream during a tournament. The player, whose name has been lost to the annals of internet shame, was a rising star in the competitive scene, known for his preternatural aim and uncanny ability to predict his opponents’ movements. During a crucial match, with thousands of viewers watching, an anti-cheat program suddenly detected a third-party application running on his computer. The game froze, a message popped up on the screen, and the player’s face went from a mask of intense concentration to a rictus of pure, unadulterated panic. He fumbled with his keyboard, desperately trying to close the offending program, but it was too late. The internet had already taken its screenshots, and his career was over before it had even truly begun. The schadenfreude was palpable. Here was a player who had not only cheated but had been caught in the most public and humiliating way possible. It was a digital Icarus, flying too close to the sun on wings of borrowed code.

And what of the toxicity? For a prime example of this, we need look no further than the world of “League of Legends,” a game so notorious for its toxic community that it has become a running joke. In 2012, a professional player named Christian “IWillDominate” Rivera was banned from the game for a year due to his persistently toxic behavior. His transgressions included, but were not limited to, a constant stream of verbal abuse, a penchant for intentionally feeding the enemy team, and a general attitude that can only be described as “aggressively unpleasant.” The case was a landmark one, as it was one of the first times that a major game developer had taken such a hard-line stance against a professional player for their in-game behavior. It sent a clear message to the community: being good at the game does not give you a free pass to be a jerk.

These are but a few of the many, many examples of cheating and toxicity in the real world of online gaming. From the highest echelons of esports to the lowest rungs of the amateur ladder, the story is the same. For every player who strives for excellence through hard work and dedication, there is another who is looking for a shortcut, a way to win without earning it. And for every player who offers a friendly “gg” at the end of a match, there is another who is composing a symphony of insults in the chat. The digital world, it seems, is just as messy and complicated as the real one. And that, in a strange way, is what makes it so endlessly fascinating.

_DISCUSSION_

The unholy alliance of cheating and toxicity in online gaming is more than just a nuisance for those seeking a fair and enjoyable experience; it is a fascinating microcosm of human behavior, a digital petri dish in which our best and worst impulses are laid bare. The implications of this correlation extend far beyond the virtual realm, offering a glimpse into the darker corners of our collective psyche and shedding light on our habits as consumers in an increasingly digital world.

At its core, the cheating-toxicity nexus is a stark reminder of the power of anonymity and the fragility of the human ego. When stripped of real-world consequences and social accountability, some individuals will inevitably gravitate towards the path of least resistance, even if that path is paved with aimbots and insults. This is not a phenomenon unique to gaming; it can be seen in the vitriolic comment sections of social media, the spread of misinformation in online forums, and the general coarsening of public discourse. The gamer who cheats their way to victory and then unleashes a torrent of abuse is the digital cousin of the Twitter troll who hides behind a fake profile picture and a string of inflammatory hashtags. Both are products of an environment that rewards outrage and de-emphasizes empathy.

From a consumer behavior perspective, the prevalence of cheating and toxicity has a profound impact on the gaming industry. It is a significant driver of player churn, with many gamers abandoning a game altogether due to a toxic community. This has created a demand for a new kind of gaming experience, one that prioritizes safety, inclusivity, and fair play. Game developers are no longer just selling a product; they are selling a community. And the health of that community is becoming an increasingly important factor in a game’s commercial success. The rise of features like honor systems, community moderation tools, and even AI-powered toxicity detectors is a direct response to this consumer demand. In a crowded marketplace, a reputation for having a non-toxic community can be a powerful competitive advantage.

Furthermore, the cheating-toxicity correlation highlights a fundamental tension in the consumer-producer relationship. Players want to win, and they will often go to great lengths to do so. Game developers, on the other hand, want to create a fair and balanced experience for all players. This creates an ongoing arms race, with developers constantly trying to patch exploits and ban cheaters, while a small but determined subset of players is constantly trying to find new ways to break the rules. It is a battle for the very soul of the game, a struggle between the desire for individual glory and the need for collective fairness. And in this battle, the consumer is not just a passive recipient of a product, but an active participant in the shaping of the experience.

_CONCLUSION_

In the grand, chaotic theater of online gaming, the cheater and the toxic player are two of the most enduring and entertaining villains. As we have seen, they are not just two separate actors on a digital stage, but a tragicomic duo, their performances inextricably linked. The cheater, with their ill-gotten victories, and the toxic player, with their venomous tirades, are two sides of the same counterfeit coin, a currency of insecurity and unearned bravado. They are a testament to the strange and wonderful ways in which the human psyche adapts, or fails to adapt, to the pressures of the digital world. And while we may never be able to fully eradicate them from our virtual playgrounds, we can at least take solace in the fact that they provide us with an endless source of amusement, a constant reminder that even in the most technologically advanced of settings, human nature remains as messy, as flawed, and as hilarious as ever. So the next time you encounter a player who is both cheating and screaming into their microphone, do not despair. Simply sit back, relax, and enjoy the show.
references:
[1] Passmore, C. J., Miller, M. K., Liu, J., & Phillips, C. J. (2020). A cheating mood: The emotional and psychological benefits of cheating in single-player games. *Proceedings of the 2020 CHI Conference on Human Factors in Computing Systems*.

[2] Irdeto. (n.d.). *Why players cheat: The psychology of unfair play*. Retrieved from https://irdeto.com/why-players-cheat-the-psychology-of-unfair-play

[3] Smith, J., Ahmed, H., Burrough, M., & Britland, I. (2024). The Psychology and Impact of Cheating. *Kennesaw State University*.

[4] Gabbiadini, A., Riva, P., Andrighetto, L., & Volpato, C. (2014). Interactive effect of moral disengagement and violent video games on self-control, cheating, and aggression. *Social Psychological and Personality Science*, *5*(4), 451-458.

[5] Cho, S. (2024). Unpacking the Dynamics of Harm in Game Cheating. *Proceedings of the ACM on Human-Computer Interaction*, *8*(CHI PLAY), 1-27.

[6] Sun, X., Yu, V., & Chen, V. H. H. (2024). Toxic behavior in multiplayer online games: the role of witnessed verbal aggression, game engagement intensity, and social self-efficacy. *Chinese Journal of Communication*, 1-19.

[7] Kordyaka, B., & Kruse, S. (2023). The cycle of toxicity: Exploring relationships between personality and player roles in toxic behavior in multiplayer online battle arena games. *Proceedings of the 2023 CHI Conference on Human Factors in Computing Systems*.
